title: The friend who asks better questions than answers
summary: Describe a friend whose value lies in the quality of the questions they ask rather than the advice they give, and what you have learned from being around them.
topic: A friend whose questions matter more than their answers
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (350–500 words) about a friend (or older relative) whose value is the questions they ask, not the advice they give, and what that style has changed about how you think. Use the present perfect for the friendship over time, a first conditional, a relative clause, and 'however' or 'although' for concession.
hintStarters: I have known Selim for almost nine years, and in that time I cannot remember a single occasion on which he has told me what to do, although…
If you bring him a problem expecting an answer, he will almost certainly disappoint you, because what he does instead is…
Although his refusal to give advice used to frustrate me, what I have slowly understood is that the questions he asks are doing something more useful, which is…

hintVocab.phrase: to take ownership of a decision
hintVocab.meaningVi: tự chịu trách nhiệm về một quyết định
phản biện một cách nhẹ nhàng
làm rõ suy nghĩ của mình
người để mình nói ý tưởng và nghe phản hồi
bàn bạc kỹ điều gì đó

sampleText:
I have known Selim for almost nine years, and in that time I cannot remember a single occasion on which he has told me what to do. He is not unhelpful; he is, by some distance, the friend I most often call when something difficult is happening. What he does is different. When I describe a problem, he tends to repeat one phrase back to me, slightly rearranged, and then ask a question I had not expected. The first time we discussed my decision to leave a job in Istanbul, I came to him with what I believed was a complaint about my manager. He listened, waited, and finally asked, 'What would you do on Monday if your manager were not the problem?' I sat with that for almost an hour, and by the end of the evening I had decided something quite different from what I had walked in believing.

If you bring Selim a problem expecting an answer, he will almost certainly disappoint you. He has a near-allergy to giving advice, which used to frustrate me far more than I admitted at the time. I would sometimes leave a conversation thinking he had withheld something useful, that a good friend should be willing to say plainly what he thought. Although that frustration has not entirely disappeared, what I have slowly come to understand is that his questions are doing something his advice never could. A piece of advice puts the decision in someone else's mouth; a good question puts the decision back where it belongs, in yours. I have watched him do this with three or four other friends over the years, and the pattern is the same: people leave his table calmer, slower, and slightly more honest with themselves than when they sat down.

What I have learned from being around him is harder to describe than a technique. It is something closer to a posture toward other people. However, I do not want to romanticise this — there are moments when what someone needs is a direct, useful answer, and a question can feel like withholding when it should be generous. The skill is knowing when each one is called for, and Selim is not always right about that either. What he has shown me, though, is that the people I have found most useful in my life have rarely been the ones who told me what to think. They have been the ones whose questions stayed in my head long after the conversation ended, working on me quietly until I noticed something I had been avoiding.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: First conditional inside reported speech
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'If you bring Selim a problem expecting an answer, he will almost certainly disappoint you' là điều kiện loại 1 — hiện tại đơn trong mệnh đề if, 'will + nguyên thể' trong mệnh đề chính — dùng để mô tả một kết quả có khả năng xảy ra trong tương lai gần.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'If you bring Selim a problem expecting an answer, he will almost certainly disappoint you' is the first conditional — present simple in the if-clause, 'will + base verb' in the main clause — used to describe a likely future outcome.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Past perfect to express a thought before a key moment
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'I had decided something quite different from what I had walked in believing' dùng quá khứ hoàn thành để diễn đạt một suy nghĩ hoặc niềm tin đã có trước thời điểm chính trong quá khứ. Cấu trúc này rất hữu ích khi kể lại sự thay đổi suy nghĩ.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'I had decided something quite different from what I had walked in believing' uses the past perfect to express a thought or belief held before the main moment in the past. This structure is very useful for narrating a change of mind.

sampleTranslationVi:
Tôi đã biết Selim gần chín năm, và trong thời gian đó tôi không thể nhớ một dịp nào mà anh ấy đã bảo tôi phải làm gì. Anh ấy không phải là người vô ích; vượt trội hơn cả, anh ấy là người bạn tôi gọi thường xuyên nhất khi có chuyện khó khăn xảy ra. Điều anh ấy làm thì khác. Khi tôi mô tả một vấn đề, anh ấy có xu hướng lặp lại một câu của tôi, được sắp xếp lại đôi chút, rồi đặt một câu hỏi tôi đã không lường trước. Lần đầu tiên chúng tôi bàn về quyết định bỏ một công việc ở Istanbul, tôi đến gặp anh ấy với điều tôi tin là một lời than phiền về quản lý của mình. Anh ấy nghe, chờ, và cuối cùng hỏi: 'Bạn sẽ làm gì vào thứ Hai nếu quản lý không phải là vấn đề?' Tôi ngồi với điều đó gần một tiếng, và đến cuối buổi tối tôi đã quyết định một điều khá khác so với điều tôi đã bước vào và tin tưởng.

Nếu bạn mang đến cho Selim một vấn đề và mong đợi một câu trả lời, anh ấy gần như chắc chắn sẽ làm bạn thất vọng. Anh ấy gần như dị ứng với việc đưa ra lời khuyên, điều từng làm tôi bực bội nhiều hơn tôi thừa nhận lúc đó. Đôi khi tôi rời cuộc trò chuyện và nghĩ rằng anh ấy đã giấu đi điều gì hữu ích, rằng một người bạn tốt nên sẵn sàng nói thẳng điều anh ấy nghĩ. Mặc dù sự bực bội đó chưa hoàn toàn biến mất, điều tôi đã dần hiểu là các câu hỏi của anh ấy đang làm điều mà lời khuyên của anh ấy không thể làm. Một lời khuyên đặt quyết định vào miệng người khác; một câu hỏi tốt đặt quyết định trở lại nơi nó thuộc về, tức là trong miệng bạn. Tôi đã chứng kiến anh ấy làm điều này với ba bốn người bạn khác trong nhiều năm, và mô hình luôn giống nhau: người ta rời bàn anh ấy bình tĩnh hơn, chậm hơn, và thành thật hơn với chính mình một chút so với khi họ ngồi xuống.

Điều tôi đã học được từ việc ở bên cạnh anh ấy khó mô tả hơn một kỹ thuật. Nó giống một thái độ đối với người khác hơn. Tuy nhiên, tôi không muốn lãng mạn hóa điều này — có những lúc điều một người cần là một câu trả lời trực tiếp, hữu ích, và một câu hỏi có thể giống như sự giữ lại trong khi đáng lẽ phải hào phóng. Kỹ năng nằm ở việc biết khi nào cần điều nào, và Selim cũng không phải lúc nào cũng đúng về điều đó. Tuy nhiên, điều anh ấy đã cho tôi thấy là những người tôi thấy hữu ích nhất trong đời mình hiếm khi là những người đã nói cho tôi biết nên nghĩ gì. Họ là những người có câu hỏi đọng lại trong đầu tôi rất lâu sau khi cuộc trò chuyện kết thúc, tác động lên tôi một cách lặng lẽ cho đến khi tôi nhận ra điều gì đó tôi đã tránh né.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the present perfect correctly to describe a friendship over time?
options: I have knew Selim for almost nine years.
I am knowing Selim for almost nine years.
I have known Selim for almost nine years.
I know Selim since almost nine years.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect 'have + past participle' is used with 'for + period' to express a state that started in the past and continues now. The past participle of 'know' is 'known'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the first conditional correctly?
options: If you bring Selim a problem expecting an answer, he would disappoint you.
If you will bring Selim a problem expecting an answer, he will disappoint you.
If you bringing Selim a problem expecting an answer, he will disappoint you.
If you bring Selim a problem expecting an answer, he will disappoint you.
mcQuestions.explanation: First conditional: present simple in the if-clause + 'will + base verb' in the main clause. 'Will' is not used inside the if-clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the past perfect correctly?
options: I had decided something quite different from what I had walked in believing.
I have decided something quite different from what I had walked in believing.
I had decide something quite different from what I had walked in believing.
I had decided something quite different from what I have walked in believing.
mcQuestions.explanation: Past perfect = 'had + past participle'. Both clauses describe actions completed before the main past moment.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Had + past participle' for actions before another past moment.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses a relative clause with 'on which' correctly?
options: I cannot remember a single occasion which on he has told me what to do.
I cannot remember a single occasion on which he has told me what to do.
I cannot remember a single occasion on which he has told me what should I do.
I cannot remember a single occasion on which has he told me what to do.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'On which' is a formal relative phrase replacing 'when'. Word order inside the relative clause is normal (subject-verb), and indirect questions use statement order ('what to do').
mcQuestions.hint: 'On which + subject + verb'; indirect questions keep statement word order.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'although' for concession?
options: Although his refusal to give advice used to frustrate me, but his questions were doing something useful.
Although, his refusal to give advice used to frustrate me his questions were doing something useful.
Although his refusal to give advice used to frustrate me, his questions were doing something useful.
His refusal to give advice used to frustrate me although however his questions were doing something useful.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + clause, main clause' — no 'but' afterwards, no comma directly after 'although', and no doubling with 'however'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'used to + base verb' for a past habit?
options: His refusal to give advice use to frustrate me.
His refusal to give advice used to frustrated me.
His refusal to give advice was used to frustrate me.
His refusal to give advice used to frustrate me.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Used to + base verb' expresses a past habit or state that no longer applies.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Used to + base verb' for past habits.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'tend to + base verb' correctly?
options: He tends to repeat one phrase back to me, slightly rearranged.
He tend to repeat one phrase back to me, slightly rearranged.
He tends repeating one phrase back to me, slightly rearranged.
He tends to repeats one phrase back to me, slightly rearranged.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Tend to + base verb' is the standard structure. With third-person singular 'he', the verb 'tend' takes -s: 'tends'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'a piece of + uncountable noun' correctly?
options: An advice puts the decision in someone else's mouth.
A piece of advice puts the decision in someone else's mouth.
A piece advice puts the decision in someone else's mouth.
A advice piece puts the decision in someone else's mouth.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Advice' is uncountable; to refer to one instance we say 'a piece of advice'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Advice' is uncountable; counter is 'a piece of advice'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'be willing to + base verb' correctly?
options: A good friend should be willing say plainly what he thought.
A good friend should be willing for saying plainly what he thought.
A good friend should be willing to say plainly what he thought.
A good friend should be willing to said plainly what he thought.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Be willing to + base verb' is the standard structure.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the comparative correctly?
options: People leave his table more calmer, more slower, and slightly more honest with themselves.
People leave his table calmer, slower, and slightly honester with themselves.
People leave his table most calm, most slow, and slightly more honest with themselves.
People leave his table calmer, slower, and slightly more honest with themselves.
mcQuestions.explanation: Short adjectives form comparatives with '-er' ('calmer', 'slower'); the longer 'honest' takes 'more honest'. No double comparatives.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'long after' correctly to mean 'a long time after'?
options: Their questions stayed in my head long after the conversation ended.
Their questions stayed in my head longer after the conversation ended.
Their questions stayed in my head long since the conversation ended.
Their questions stayed in my head long until the conversation ended.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Long after + event' = 'a long time after the event'. 'Longer after', 'long since' and 'long until' do not fit.

criticalThinkingQuestion: If the friend who asks better questions changes what you decide, but the friend who gives a direct answer saves you time, which one do you actually want when the stakes are high? Why?
